coredata.dc:title: Photocatalysis of an organic pollutant using ZnOsemiconductor nanoparticles embedded biogenic nitrogen doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Dhanush, Chandrasekaran
Ismayati, Maya
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent natural carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized using Ixora coccinea flower extract. These amorphous NCDs, around 3 nm in size, were characterized by FT-IR, confirming the presence of functional groups such as –OH, C–H, C=O, and C-N. Optical properties of the NCDs were explored through UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. A ZnO-NPs@NCDs composite was prepared, with a band-gap value of 3.58 eV, indicating its UV responsiveness. FT-IR analysis revealed carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups in the composite. XRD confirmed the hexagonal Wurtzite phase of ZnO, with an average particle size of 36.42 nm, determined by Scherrer’s equation. Raman studies showed a reduction in amorphous nature due to the integration of crystalline ZnO-NPs. XPS analysis detailed the chemical composition and elemental states of the composite. ZnO-NPs@NCDs acted as an efficient catalyst in the photocatalytic degradation of MO dye, achieving 95 % degradation within 50 min, due to enhanced synergistic effects and interfacial interactions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent natural carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized using Ixora coccinea flower extract. These amorphous NCDs, around 3 nm in size, were characterized by FT-IR, confirming the presence of functional groups such as –OH, C–H, C=O, and C-N. Optical properties of the NCDs were explored through UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. A ZnO-NPs@NCDs composite was prepared, with a band-gap value of 3.58 eV, indicating its UV responsiveness. FT-IR analysis revealed carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups in the composite. XRD confirmed the hexagonal Wurtzite phase of ZnO, with an average particle size of 36.42 nm, determined by Scherrer’s equation. Raman studies showed a reduction in amorphous nature due to the integration of crystalline ZnO-NPs. XPS analysis detailed the chemical composition and elemental states of the composite. ZnO-NPs@NCDs acted as an efficient catalyst in the photocatalytic degradation of MO dye, achieving 95 % degradation within 50 min, due to enhanced synergistic effects and interfacial interactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of carbon-related nanoparticles with sizes smaller than 10 nm, and they were discovered in the early years of the 21st century [1]. CDs display remarkable properties and find diverse applications in catalysis, bio-imaging, sensing, fabricating energy storage devices, optoelectronics and theranostics [2]. CDs are zero dimensional nanomaterials and attract attention due to their non-toxicity, cost effectiveness, eco-friendly methods of synthesis, high chemical stability, superior water solubility, tunable-fluorescent behavior, good bio-compatibility and easy functionalization [3]. CDs and CDs doped with heteroatoms such as N, B, S and P are synthesized using various methods including electrochemical synthesis, acid oxidation, hydrothermal synthesis, arc discharge, laser ablation and microwave synthesis [4]. Amidst them, hydrothermal approach is mostly preferred since it is more cost-effective, easy workable especially when natural extracts (with required phyto-constituents) were employed as precursors for the synthesis of CDs.

In addition, CDs are used as a catalyst in effluents dye degradation studies also. Whereas in the current era, water pollution has become the greatest environmental menace worldwide in the view of increased industrialization [5]. Water pollution is also a critical concern as it directly impacts aquatic ecosystems, allowing contaminants to enter the food chain and ultimately reach humans. Some water pollutants arise from domestic, industrial, or agricultural activities through the transformation of naturally occurring compounds whereas others are entirely synthetic, created by human activities and absent in nature. For example, the production of surfactants, pesticides, petrochemicals, plastics, and organic dyes has introduced numerous chemicals into the environment, posing significant ecological challenges [6–8]. Synthetic organic dye effluents from the industries discharged into the water bodies inflict harm to aquatic life directly and other living beings indirectly [9]. Most of these effluents seems to be toxic and they change the metabolism of the living beings. Some of the dye molecules are highly stable due to their complex structure and they are non-biodegradable too. Degradation of the complex dye molecules and removal of heavy metals are very much necessary before discharging the effluents into the water bodies for their conservation [10–12]. Techniques including chemical precipitation, electrochemical destruction, sedimentation, adsorption, ion exchange, and photocatalytic methods have been employed for dye degradation to reduce water pollution. Among these, photocatalytic degradation is recognized as a simple, efficient, energy-saving, and environmentally friendly approach for treating sewage and sludge. Aforesaid features attracted most of the researchers to concentrate on photocatalytic degradation method [13]. Methyl orange (MO) is one of the coloured organic dyes mostly used for dyeing silk, wool, and cotton fabric materials. When MO dye encounters human beings, it causes eye and skin irritations. In case if it is swallowed, it causes irritation in respiratory and gastrointestinal track leading to nausea, vomiting and diarrhea [14]. MO dye is highly soluble in water but less biodegradable which makes it so difficult to separate from the water bodies when they are mixed. The distinctive properties of MO dye stem from the presence of the azo group (N=N) within its structure. The contaminated MO in water could absorb or reflect sunlight and so the biological activities such as bacterial growth gets very much affected. Waste water, containing MO dye, undergoes many chemical and biological changes and consumes dissolved oxygen from the stream destroying the life of aquatic animals and plants. MO dye causes micro-toxicity to fish and other organisms by undergoing chelation with metal ions. This dye is considered as a major water pollutant because it causes abnormal coloration to the surface waters. This colored water causes adverse pollution, eutrophication and perturbations in aquatic life [15]. Numerous methods are being developed for the eviction of dye materials from water among which photocatalytic degradation of dyes is quite popular [16]. Moreover, the use of semiconducting materials as photocatalysts enhances the efficiency of the photocatalytic degradation process. Semiconducting materials such as ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs) with a band gap energy of 3.3 eV were employed as photocatalysts due to their wider band gap energy, increased binding energy, and effective absorption of light energy [17,18]. A significant challenge in achieving high photocatalytic efficiency lies in the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, which severely impacts the quantum efficiency of photocatalysis. To address this issue, various strategies have been explored, including metal doping, noble metal deposition, semiconductor coupling, dye sensitization, and non-metal modifications [19]. To improve the photocatalytic efficiency of ZnO-NPs, carbon-based materials have been coupled with them. These carbon-based materials, along with metal nanoparticles, have been extensively utilized to effectively degrade harmful dyes under UV light exposure. The synergistic interaction between ZnO-NPs and carbon enhances the photosensitivity of the carbon-based ZnO-NPs photocatalyst [20–24].

In this study, carbon was sourced from Ixora coccinea flower extract (ICFE). Ixora coccinea, commonly referred to as jungle geranium, flame of the woods, or jungle flame, belongs to the Rubiaceae family of flowering plants. Phytoconstituents found in Ixora coccinea include lupeol, ursolic acid, oleanolic acid, sitosterol, rutin, lecocyanadin, anthocyanins, proanthocyanidins and glycosides of kaempferol and quercetin [25]. Ixora coccinea is a commercially significant medicinal plant utilized in Ayurveda. Various parts of the plant, including the flower, leaf, and root, are employed in medicinal preparations for conditions such as skin diseases and diabetes. Additionally, it is used in the treatment of leucorrhoea, dysentery, dysmenorrhoea, haemoptysis, hypertension, menstrual irregularities, sprains, bronchitis fever, sores, chronic ulcers, scabies and other skin diseases [26].

The novelty of this research lies in the development of a highly efficient photocatalyst (ZnO-NPs@NCDs) utilizing ZnO-NPs embedded with biogenic NCDs prepared from ICFE. This innovative approach combines the superior photocatalytic properties of ZnO with the enhanced charge separation and band gap modulation imparted by biogenic NCDs, enabling effective and eco-friendly degradation of the organic pollutant MO under UV light irradiation. The use of biogenic NCDs not only promotes sustainability but also enhances the system's performance, providing a unique advancement over traditional photocatalytic materials.
Experimental (Refer supplementary document):

The HR-TEM profile image of NCDs is depicted in Fig. 1 a. NCDs exhibited monodispersity, spherical morphology, and possessed a particle size of approximately 3 nm in diameter, with a size distribution ranging from 1 to 5 nm (Fig. 1b). The diffused SAED ring patterns of NCDs indicate their amorphous nature (Fig. 1c) [27] .

The NCDs derived from ICFE underwent XRD analysis, and the XRD spectrum is presented in Fig. 2 . A broad peak around 25.17° and a faint peak at approximately 40.68° were attributed to the (002) and (100) planes, respectively. The broad peak at 25.17° indicated the low crystallinity of the synthesized NCDs, suggesting their amorphous nature and random arrangement based on JCPDS Card No. 41–1487 [28]. The calculated d-spacing values for the (002) and (100) planes were 0.331 nm and 0.221 nm, respectively. These values suggest the presence of slightly distorted carbon structures, likely due to the inclusion of carbon and nitrogen-containing functional groups within the carbon framework [29,30].

The FT-IR spectroscopic analysis of NCDs (Fig. 3 ) indicated the existence of nitrogen and functional groups containing oxygen. These groups originate from the ICFE and aqueous NH3, respectively. A broad band at approximately 3380 cm−1 corresponds to the surface stretching vibrations of carboxylic and hydroxyl moieties (–OH/–NH) and may also include vibrations from adsorbed water molecules. The asymmetric stretching vibration noticed at 2927 cm−1 is attributed to the C–H bond. A weak band around 1745 cm−1 shows the presence of C=O groups. Five sharp bands located at approximately 1639 cm−1, 1421 cm−1, 1282 cm−1, 1159 cm−1, and 664 cm−1 confirm the existence of various bonds, including C=C stretching vibrations, C-N stretching vibrations, stretching vibrations of C-O bonds, formation of C-N-C/C-O-C bonds and C–H (aromatic structures) bonds respectively. Therefore, the FT-IR analysis confirms the existence of –OH/–NH, C–H, C=O, C=C, C-N, C-O, C-N-C/C-O-C, and C–H bonds in the NCDs [31–34].

UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques were employed to analyse the optical properties of NCDs. Fig. 4 a illustrates the UV–Visible spectrum of NCDs. In this spectrum, a broad and intense shoulder was observed at 272 nm, while a weaker broad shoulder was noted around 305 nm. The shoulder at 272 nm suggests the π-π* transition of the in-plane sp2 hybridized honeycomb-like carbon structures (C=C). The weak shoulder at 305 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of groups containing heteroatoms such as C=N/C=O within the carbonaceous structure [35,36].

Fig. 5 (a) & (b) illustrate the fluorescence spectra of NCDs. Under UV light (λex = 365 nm), NCDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence, while under sunlight, they appeared as a transparent pale yellowish-brown colour, attributable to the presence of CDs. A prominent blue emission was observed at 400 nm when excited at 320 nm, indicating the π*-π transition in NCDs. Furthermore, a gradual decrease in fluorescence emission with a red shift was observed as the excitation wavelengths were increased from 330 to 420 nm, attributed to the π*-n transition. This variation in fluorescence emission is generally attributed to differences in the nano sizes of the fluorescent species in the sample, and it is also believed that nitrogen doping influences the fluorescence behaviour of the NCDs [37,38]. In Fig. 4b, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of NCDs is depicted. A fluorescence excitation shoulder at 320 nm was observed, corresponding to an emission wavelength of 400 nm. These observations confirm that NCDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission behaviour [39].

Fig. 6 illustrates the zeta potential distribution of the NCDs synthesized from ICFE. The zeta potential value is 0.0695 mV. Analysis of the zeta potential indicates that the synthesized NCDs from ICFE exhibit stability. This zeta value is attributed to the presence of a greater number of oxygen-containing –CO and –OH groups on the facade of NCDs. These functional groups, namely –CO and –OH, are the primary contributors to the hydrophilous nature of the NCDs and their outstanding water miscibility. Consequently, the synthesized NCDs demonstrate increased stability, less aggregation and high solubility in aqueous media [40].

SEM is employed to examine particle morphology by scanning the surface with a focused electron beam. The morphology of the synthesized ZnO-NPs was investigated using SEM at a magnification of 5.00 kx. The nanoparticles were observed to have an irregular and nearly spherical shape (Fig. 7 ). Some particles were observed to cluster together due to agglomeration caused by weak physical forces between the formed nanoparticles [41–43].

SEM analysis is utilized to characterize the surface morphology, structure, shape, and orientations of composite materials. Fig. 8 presents SEM images, elemental mapping, and EDS spectrum of ZnO-NPs@NCDs. The nano-sized particles were homogeneously distributed on the carbon layer surface, as clearly observed in Fig. 8a-c. ZnO-NPs on the carbon layers exhibited irregular structures. The presence of elements in the ZnO-NPs@NCDs was confirmed through elemental mapping and the EDS spectrum (Fig. 8d-h). The EDS spectrum revealed that ZnO-NPs@NCDs contained 63.01 % carbon, 19.17 % nitrogen, 13.21 % oxygen, and 4.61 % zinc. The primary source of the carbon signal was originated from the carbon layers derived from ICFE. While residual nitrogen, oxygen-containing functional moieties of the NCDs, and ZnO contributed to the presence of nitrogen, oxygen, and zinc. The nonexistence of other elements in the EDS spectrum indicates, the synthesized ZnO-NPs@NCDs is free from external impurities [44–46].

Ultraviolet- Diffused Reflectance Spectroscopic (UV-DRS) study was carried out to determine the optical bandgap of ZnO-NPs@NCDs by Tauc plot.
(1)

Equation (1) depicts Tauc’s expression, where “α” is the absorption coefficient, “hυ” is the frequency of incident light, “A” is a proportionality constant, and “Eg” is the band gap. In the Tauc plot, the x-axis represents energy, while the y-axis represents the (αhυ)2 parameters. The bandgap of the material is indicated by the intercept of a straight line along the linear part of the curve with the hυ axis [44,47]. In this method, ZnO-NPs@NCDs exhibited two absorption shoulders at 230 nm and 274 nm. The existence of ZnO-NPs notably influenced the optical properties of NCDs, as evidenced by the shift in the absorption shoulders of ZnO-NPs@NCDs from 272 nm and 305 nm to 230 nm and 274 nm. The shift indicates strong electronic interaction between ZnO-NPs and NCDs, suggesting enhanced charge transfer processes [48]. In addition, this absorption occurred is also because of electrons being excited from the valence band of Zn to their corresponding conduction band (CB). Fig. 9 illustrates the Tauc plot of ZnO-NPs@NCDs, with the derived bandgap value from the plot being 3.58 eV [49].

The FT-IR spectrum of ZnO-NPs@NCDs is depicted in Fig. 10 , providing insights into the surface functionalization of ZnO-NPs@NCDs. As illustrated in Fig. 10, the stretching vibration band of –OH/–NH groups were observed around 3426 cm−1, while the C–H asymmetric stretching vibration band appeared at 2933 cm−1. A band at approximately 1734 cm−1 signalled the presence of the C=O functional moiety. Another band at 1624 cm−1 corresponded to the sp2 hybridized honeycomb graphitic carbon structure (C=C). Stretching vibrations of C-N heteroatoms were observed around 1374 cm−1, indicating the presence of nitrogen-containing molecules on the carbonaceous layer. Additionally, a vibration band between 1245 cm−1 and 1077 cm−1 confirmed the existence of C-O-C (epoxy) functional moieties. Furthermore, a band around 606 cm−1 could be assigned to the Zn-O vibration, suggesting that the synthesized ZnO-NPs were physically adsorbed on the carbon layer, forming complexes with oxygen. Additionally, a significant shift in the C=O stretching vibrations (from 1701 cm−1 for NCDs to 1734 cm−1 for ZnO-NPs@NCDs) and a decrease in intensity were observed in ZnO-NPs@NCDs. This change was attributed to the interaction between zinc and the carbonyl group, confirming the incorporation of ZnO-NPs onto the carbon surface [50]. Therefore, the existence of functional groups such as –CO and –OH groups on the synthesized ZnO-NPs@NCDs was verified through FT-IR analysis [1,51,52].

XRD is a key methodology for determining the crystalline or amorphous nature of the synthesized materials. The wide-angle range powder XRD pattern of ZnO-NPs@NCDs is presented in Fig. 11 . The synthesized ZnO-NPs@NCDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak around 24.50° for C (002), with a corresponding d-spacing value for the plane of 0.334 nm (calculated from the Bragg equation), indicating the presence of NCDs in ZnO-NPs@NCDs. Distinctive peaks observed at 31.72°, 34.27°, 36.11°, 47.42°, 56.49°, 62.60°, 66.30°, 67.85° and 69.18° were attributed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201) planes, respectively, of the Wurtzite phase of ZnO (hexagonal), and they matched well with JCPDS No. 36–1451. The sharp, prominent peaks at the (100) and (101) planes confirmed the crystallinity of ZnO-NPs [53–55].
The Scherrer’s equation (D = Kλ /β cos θ) was employed to calculate the average size of the ZnO-NPs within the ZnO-NPs@NCDs composite, resulting in an estimated size of approximately 36.42 nm.

Here, β denotes the full width at half maximum (FWHM) in radians, K denotes the dimensionless shape factor, typically approaching unity, λ signifies the incident X-ray wavelength in nm (0.154 nm), θ denotes the peak position in radians and D stands for the average size in nm of the crystalline domains [56].

The Raman spectrum of ZnO-NPs@NCDs exhibited two distinct peaks at 1367 cm−1 (D band) and 1603 cm−1 (G band), corresponding to defective carbon (disordered) and graphitic carbon (ordered), respectively (Fig. 12 ). The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of ZnO-NPs@NCDs was measured at 0.53, indicating a reduction in the amorphous nature of the composite due to the inclusion of crystalline ZnO-NPs. Additionally, small peaks were observed around 600–900 cm−1 in the spectrum, attributed to the characteristic hexagonal Wurtzite phase of ZnO-NPs. Consequently, the results from XRD and Raman spectra confirm the manifestation of ZnO-NPs and graphene-like carbon structures in ZnO-NPs@NCDs, which were found to be free from impurities [57,58].

HR-TEM images of ZnO-NPs@NCDs are displayed in Fig. 13 (a & b). The uniform dispersion of ZnO-NPs on the carbonaceous matrix minimizes aggregation, as evidenced by the absence of large clusters. The carbon layer appears porous, providing a supportive scaffold that enhances nanoparticles stability and dispersion. Fig. 13c represents the clear lattice fringes of the ZnO-NPs@NCDs with 2.82 Å corresponding (100) plane of hexagonal ZnO [55,59]. Additionally, the SAED pattern ( Fig. 13d) reveals distinct diffraction rings, further confirming the crystalline nature of ZnO-NPs and their successful integration onto the carbon layer. The interface between ZnO-NPs and the carbon matrix is crucial for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. This synergy stems from the intimate contact, facilitating effective charge transfer and reducing electron-hole recombination. The high degree of dispersion ensures maximum exposure of active sites, contributing to the observed photocatalytic performance. This shows the significant morphology of ZnO-NPs over the carbon surface in ZnO-NPs@NCDs.

XPS characterization technique revealed the chemical composition and the elemental states of the ZnO-NPs@NCDs (Fig. 15 ). Survey scan spectrum (Fig. 15a) showed independent peaks of C1s (282.4 eV), N1s (397.6 eV), O1s (529.6 eV) and Zn2p (1018.4 eV) in ZnO-NPs@NCDs. Deconvoluted C1s spectra (Fig. 15b) exhibited three peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.15 eV and 292.25 eV evidencing the existence of C=C (sp2), C-O/C-N carbon bonds and π electron delocalization (satellite peak) respectively [61]. Deconvoluted N1s spectra (Fig. 15c) peak at 398.23 eV, 399.18 eV and 400 eV were due to the existence of C-N-C, N-(C)3 and N–H bonds accordingly [62–64]. Three elemental bonding states of O1s were revealed from the deconvoluted curves of O1s (Fig. 15d) which showed the presence of C-OH, C-N-O/C=O and C-O-C/Zn-O at 530.18 eV, 531.43 eV and 532.23 eV respectively. Two clear deconvoluted curves of Zn2p spectra (Fig. 15e) at 1019.35 eV and 1041.95 eV correspond to the Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 states respectively [65]. Thus, it was clear from the XPS results that ZnO-NPs were physically adsorbed on carbon layers and nitrogen was chemically bound over the carbon layers. The aforesaid explanations were consistent with the FT-IR results. Finally, the synthesized ZnO-NPs@NCDs was utilized as a photo-catalyst for the degradation of MO dye under UV light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of the synthesized ZnO-NPs@NCDs in the photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) dye under UV light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of ZnO-NPs@NCDs was assessed by degrading methyl orange (MO) dye under UV light illumination and it was monitored spectroscopically. The intensity of the absorption peak of MO dye at approximately 450 nm was measured to evaluate the photocatalytic activity. Prior to initiate the degradation study, the reaction mixture was agitated in darkness for 60 min to achieve an equilibrium between adsorption and desorption between the MO dye and ZnO-NPs@NCDs. In the presence of ZnO-NPs@NCDs (Bandgap: 3.58 eV), a gradual diminution in the absorption intensity of the MO dye was observed upon illumination with UV light, monitored at regular intervals (every 10 min). Approximately 95 % degradation was achieved within 50 min (Fig. 16 a). However, in the absence of ZnO-NPs@NCDs (i.e., illumination under UV light alone), only about 12 % degradation was observed at 50 min, and no significant decrease in absorption intensity was observed even after increasing the illumination time up to 180 min.

Under the same UV- light illumination, bare NCDs and ZnO-NPs were used separately as a catalyst in the photocatalytic degradation of MO dye to assess their respective photocatalytic efficiency. As a result, bare NCDs exhibited only 10–20 % degradation and ZnO-NPs exhibited 55 % degradation at 50 min respectively (Fig. 16b). ZnO-NPs@NCDs reaction followed pseudo-first order kinetics [59] and the kinetic parameters like rate constant (k) = 0.1799 cm−1, slope = -0.049 and R2 = 0.9908 were also measured (Fig. 16c) [66].

The catalytic effectiveness of ZnO-NPs@NCDs primarily stemmed from the distribution of ZnO-NPs and functional groups on the carbon surface. Expansive surface area of the ZnO-NPs@NCDs facilitated efficient access for MO dye molecules, enhancing the photocatalytic system's performance [58]. In the case of bare NCDs, there was a decent electron-transfer process between MO dye and NCDs. However, a sluggish reaction rate ensued due to electron accumulation on the NCDs surface, low bandgap and rapid recombination of electron-active dye, resulted in inefficient photocatalytic degradation of MO dye [67]. However, ZnO-NPs with an optimal bandgap (approximately 3 eV) degraded MO dye by up to 55 % within 50 min. Hence, the integration of a ZnO-NPs layer onto NCDs significantly contributes to enhance the photocatalytic efficacy of ZnO-NPs@NCDs [52]. Fig. 16d shows the graphical representation of UV-light illumination photocatalytic degradation of MO dye.

The specific surface area of the synthesized ZnO-NPs and ZnO-NPs@NCDs were identified using N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm shown in the Fig. S3. Both the materials exhibited Type-IV isotherm characteristic of mesoporous materials. After the incorporation of NCDs, the SBET increased from 53.83 m2/g to 109.62 m2/g. Thus, the synthesized ZnO-NPs@NCDs results to be utilized as an efficient photocatalyst [68,69]. The photodegradation mechanism can be elucidated as follows: Upon UV light exposure, electrons in the valence band (VB) of ZnO-NPs undergoes excitation to the conduction band (CB), constructing electron-hole pairs (h+) in the VB. Subsequently, the generated e-s migrate to the CB of the carbon layers, where they are discharged, triggering the formation of superoxide anion radicals (O2 .-) upon reaction with oxygen (O2). Meanwhile, the rapid separation of h+ leads to their reaction with water molecules, yielding hydroxyl radicals (.OH). These freshly formed radicals (.OH and O2 .-) collaborate in the degradation of MO dye adsorbed on ZnO-NPs@NCDs. Consequently, the carbon surface of ZnO-NPs@NCDs serves as an effective electron aggregator and carrier, bolstering the photocatalytic activity of ZnO-NPs@NCDs under UV light. Furthermore, the introduction of ZnO-NPs onto the carbon surface enhances synergistic effects and interfacial interactions, thereby facilitating the efficient photocatalytic degradation of MO dye [70–72]. Scavenger test and the role of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) (Fig. S4) is fostered in the supplementary material [73]. The corresponding fragmentation scheme and degraded mass fragments spectra are shown in Fig. S1. and Fig. S2 (a-g) [74]. Equation 2–5 exhibits the photocatalytic degradation sequence. Table 1 shows the comparison of ZnO-NPs@NCDs composite’s degradation efficiency with respect to the past works.
(2) ZnO + hν → ZnO (CBe−+VBh+)---------------
(3) ZnO(e−) + O2 → O2 .−+ ZnO---------------
(4) ZnO(h+) + H2O → .OH + H++ ZnO---------------
(5) .OH + O2 .− + MO Dye → Degraded products (CO2↑ + H2O)---------------

In this study, eco-friendly NCDs and ZnO-NPs@NCDs composite were synthesized through hydrothermal carbonization using the flower extract of Ixora coccinea. The synthesized NCDs possessed unique optical properties with blue emission at 400 nm. HR-TEM data proved that NCDs were ∼ 3 nm in size. NCDs possessed carbonyl, hydroxyl and amine functional moieties and it was evident from the FT-IR data. SAED and XRD data explained the amorphous nature of NCDs. ZnO-NPs@NCDs composite was found to possess crystalline nature when compared with NCDs. XRD, Raman and SAED results showed their characteristic crystalline nature. The presence of elemental constituents and their respective proportions were confirmed through FT-IR, EDAX, and XPS analysis. A bandgap value of 3.58 eV was determined from the Tauc plot of ZnO-NPs@NCDs, indicating its potential as an effective photocatalyst for degrading MO dye under UV light irradiation. In the presence of ZnO-NPs@NCDs, approximately 95 % degradation of the dye was achieved within 50 min, compared to only about 12 % degradation in its absence. Thus, it is apparent that ZnO-NPs supported NCDs exhibit higher degradation efficiency compared to standalone ZnO-NPs and NCDs. Future studies focuses on optimizing the synthesis process to improve photocatalytic efficiency under other light sources. Investigating the degradation of other industrial pollutants and scaling up the synthesis for industrial applications would also be valuable. Moreover, incorporating other biogenic materials and exploring synergistic effects with other semiconductor materials could open new avenues for enhancing photocatalytic systems.
